dialogue.utterance: Dispatch to Unit 3 responding for chest pain.
Hi, I'm Alex, an EMT with the rescue squad. What made you call 911 today?
Uh, shortness of breath that began around 10 PM while I was sleeping and woke me up.
I see. Have you experienced chest pain or any other discomfort with this shortness of breath?
No, just the shortness of breath.
Are you alert and oriented to person, place, time, and situation?
Yes, I think so.
You're alert and oriented to person, place, time, and situation (AOx4). Can you take a deep breath for me?
We're checking your breathing rate and depth. You're breathing at 24-fast and labored with accessory muscle use and grunting.
We're also checking your pulse. It's elevated at 91 right now, but strong and regular.
Your blood pressure is high, at 227 over 135.
Your oxygen saturation is at 93%. We'll try to increase it for you.
Your airway is patent and self-maintained. Your breathing is fast, at 24-36 breaths per minute, adequate in depth, but labored.
Your blood glucose is currently undetectable.
We've noted clear lung sounds initially, but now we hear rales at the posterior lung bases and occasional expiratory wheezing.
A 12-lead EKG was done and it shows sinus tachycardia, the same as before.
A 4-lead monitor is in place and we see the same findings-sinus rhythm or sinus tachycardia, no life-threatening arrhythmias.
For your comfort, we'll attempt a non-rebreather mask at 15 LPM to see if it helps you breathe better.
I can't hold it near me, I feel like I can't breathe, it's suffocating me!
Let's go back to nasal cannula at 6 LPM, which we found brings your saturation up to 94-95%.
Your heart rate is now 104 with a rhythm of sinus tachycardia.
Your breathing rate is 23, still tachypneic.
Your blood pressure has dropped slightly to 134 over 85.
Your oxygen saturation is 87 now, but you continue to tell us that you can't catch your breath.
Shortly after, your blood pressure spikes again to 234 over 135, with a heart rate of 103, but your oxygen saturation rose back to 95%.
Your skin is normal in color, warm, and dry. Your peripheral pulses are strong and regular.
Let me review your medical history. You have pulmonary hypertension, anemia, end-stage renal disease with dialysis, and a history of opioid use.
Your last dialysis was Saturday and you confirmed that you haven't missed any recent sessions.
You also stated that you've been taking your prescribed medications regularly.
Based on your history and vitals, we've been following the respiratory distress protocol (Protocol 3-11).
Your skin is intact-no rashes, bruises, or other visible issues.
We didn't find any trauma to your head or neck.
You are using accessory muscles to breathe and have trouble forming full sentences.
Your mouth is clear-no discharge or obstruction noted.
Rales remain in the lung bases and there are occasional wheezing sounds.
No visible signs of abdominal pain or tenderness reported.
You don't show any weakness or deformity. Range of motion in the extremities is intact.
You are fully alert and oriented. GCS is 15 (E4, V5, M6).
There was no loss of function or targeted inactivity. You haven't refused movement during our exams.
Another 12-lead EKG shows no significant changes. We've seen sinus tachycardia only.
You're not bleeding and no other signs of life-threatening issues are present beyond your breathing difficulty.
We'll transport you to [COMPANY_NAME] now. The emergency room has been notified.
We'll help you onto the stretcher now.
You are now positioned for comfort on the stretcher, covered with a sheet, and secured with five-point restraints.
I'll continue monitoring and updating the hospital en route with any changes in your condition.
The cardiac monitor will stay on. It continues to show sinus rhythm or tachycardia only.
Your breathing is 24 again, still labored, but we're keeping you as comfortable as possible.
You are secured with five-point restraints, covered with a sheet, and ready for transport.
If you have any need at the hospital, just let me know before we arrive.
I'll give the ER a full report on your condition, including your refusal of CPAP and the oxygen tolerance level we found.
Everything checks out for transport. You appear stable, and the hospital is aware we are coming.
I'll continue checking your pulse ox, respiratory rate, and blood pressure every five minutes en route.
Your heart rate is still 104, and you remain in tachycardia.
Oxygen levels remain stable at 94-95% with nasal cannula.
No significant worsening of your condition at this time. Breathing remains as expected from pre-transport observations.
We're now outside the emergency department. The ER knows we're arriving and will be ready to take over.
You're headed to [COMPANY_NAME] in the ER. They've been provided with your medical details and EMS treatment.
Your reported pain level is zero, and your breathing hasn't worsened since we started care.
No signs of worsening pain during interventions or during our preparation for transport.
I'll do another full set of vitals per protocol while on the way to ensure we catch any changes.
I'll keep your BP, pulse, and oxygen saturation checked every five minutes as required.
